output:
**Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.**

Data-driven discovery of the governing equations of a nonlinear dynamical system has, since 2011, rested on one paradigm: expand the velocity field (or map) in a *fixed* dictionary — a truncated power series or Fourier series — and recover the expansion-coefficient vector by sparse optimization / compressive sensing. This works beautifully when the true system is sparse in that dictionary (Lorenz, Rössler, the standard map) and collapses completely when it is not. The canonical wall is the Ikeda map of a laser pulse in an optical cavity, whose components are cos/sin of a rational phase k/(1+x²+y²) and therefore expand into *infinitely many* power-series terms: the coefficient vector is dense, and sparse optimization has nothing to exploit. A second, quieter failure is hidden in the same formulation: compressive-sensing recovery guarantees require the measurement (projection) matrix to be *random and incoherent* (Gaussian-like, restricted-isometry-satisfying), yet the power-series design matrix built by evaluating monomials along a single trajectory is highly *structured and coherent* — so even a sparse coefficient vector may be unrecoverable by ℓ1. The field has treated "the system is dense" as a verdict of intrinsic unlearnability. This proposal argues that verdict is premature: density is a property of the *chosen basis*, not of the system, and the recovery failure is the superposition of two separable, individually-attackable defects.

---

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can the governing equations of a nonlinear dynamical system whose expansion is dense in every fixed polynomial or Fourier basis — the regime where compressive-sensing equation discovery provably fails, exemplified by the Ikeda map — be recovered by *discovering* a data-adaptive composed dictionary in which the system is sparse, while replacing the violated random-projection (restricted-isometry) requirement with a coherence-aware recovery guarantee stated in terms of the structured design matrix's mutual coherence?

---

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

The sparse-optimization paradigm for finding equations of motion from data (Wang, Yang, Lai, Kovanis & Grebogi, *PRL* 2011; Brunton, Proctor & Kutz, SINDy, *PNAS* 2016) is one of the few tools that returns an *interpretable, mechanistic* model — an actual differential equation or map — rather than a black-box predictor. Its reach defines the reach of interpretable system identification. Today that reach stops at an artificially low ceiling: any system that is not sparse in a hand-chosen power or Fourier basis is declared out of scope. Closing the dense-coefficient gap matters on four distinct dimensions.

**First, it converts a binary verdict into a measurable landscape.** The literature speaks of systems for which "sparse optimization is effective" versus those for which it "will fail," with the Ikeda map as the standard example of the latter. But "dense" is not an intrinsic label — the Ikeda map is dense in monomials and *would be sparse* if the right composed atoms (trigonometric functions of a rational argument) were available. By introducing a **basis-density spectrum** (model density as a function of dictionary family) and a **coherence meter** μ(Θ) (how far the data design matrix is from the random ideal compressive sensing assumes), the proposal replaces the dense/sparse dichotomy with two continuous, estimable axes. A practitioner could, for the first time, *diagnose* why discovery failed — wrong basis, coherent measurements, or genuine unidentifiability — instead of abandoning the system.

**Second, it directly enlarges the class of physically important systems amenable to white-box discovery.** Rational and trig-of-rational nonlinearities are not exotic: they appear in optics (the Ikeda cavity map itself), in gravitational and Coulomb-like interactions (1/r potentials), in enzyme kinetics (Michaelis–Menten rational rates), in power-grid swing equations (sinusoidal coupling), and in any system with saturation or normalization. These are precisely the systems where today one must fall back to a non-interpretable surrogate (a reservoir computer or a neural ODE) that forecasts but does not *explain*. A method that recovers compact symbolic structure for the dense class would let the interpretable-discovery program follow the model-free forecasting program into the regimes it currently cedes.

**Third, it repairs a foundational gap in the theory, not just the practice.** The Introduction's own observation — that compressive sensing assumes a random projection matrix while the power-series formulation produces a structured, correlated one — has been largely unaddressed in the dynamics literature, even though the corresponding theory exists in signal processing (mutual-coherence recovery bounds; Donoho & Elad, *PNAS* 2003; Tropp, *IEEE T-IT* 2004). Importing and adapting coherence-based recovery to trajectory-built dictionaries, and *measuring* whether discovered systems live below the coherence threshold s < ½(1 + 1/μ), would give the field a recovery guarantee that actually matches its measurement model. This reframes a decade of empirical "it worked / it didn't" reports around a checkable sufficient condition.

**Fourth, it forces the community to confront model non-uniqueness honestly.** Once one searches over *which basis* to use, many distinct compact models can fit the same trajectory equally well in-sample — a non-uniqueness that fixed-basis sparse optimization hides by construction. A discovery method that reports a *single* equation while silently discarding equally-good alternatives is epistemically dangerous, especially for the high-stakes forecasting (climate tipping points, laser-cavity stability) these tools are increasingly used for. By making invariant-consistency (largest Lyapunov exponent, correlation dimension, power spectrum) the model-selection criterion, the proposal turns non-uniqueness from a hidden liability into an explicit, auditable selection step — a methodological contribution reusable by any equation-discovery pipeline, sparse or dense.

A paper that establishes these four points would not merely add a trick for one hard map; it would re-found data-driven equation discovery on basis-and-measurement *co-design* with a matched recovery theory, and hand the field a diagnostic vocabulary (basis-density spectrum, coherence meter, invariant-consistency selection) that outlives the specific method.

---

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The problem sits at the intersection of six hardness axes; each defeats a naive fix, and together they rule out every obvious shortcut.

**1. The Ikeda obstruction is transcendental, not merely high-order, so "use a bigger dictionary" cannot work.** A tempting first move is to add more power-series terms. But the Ikeda components are cos and sin of a rational phase φ = p − k/(1+x²+y²); expanded in x and y they contain a *countably infinite* number of monomials with non-decaying structure. No finite polynomial or finite Fourier-in-(x,y) truncation is sparse, and increasing the truncation order only inflates the dictionary while the coefficient vector stays dense — worsening both the conditioning and the coherence. Even a *rational* dictionary (ratios of polynomials, as in implicit SINDy) does not make the Ikeda map exactly sparse, because cos(rational) is not itself rational. The correct sparsifying atoms are *compositions* — trigonometric functions of a learned rational feature — which lie outside every standard fixed dictionary. The search is therefore over a combinatorial space of *composed* functional forms, not over coefficients in a known basis.

**2. The differentiable search over bases is non-convex and prone to degenerate solutions.** Replacing a fixed basis with a learned lifting φ_θ(x) (a small network or random-feature map) and demanding sparsity in the readout makes the problem jointly non-convex in (θ, coefficients). Two degeneracies lurk. (a) The lifting can *memorize* the trajectory — fitting the data with a flexible φ_θ that has no compact symbolic interpretation, recovering the predictive power of a neural ODE while losing the entire point (interpretability). (b) The trivial sparsifier: a lifting that outputs the next-state value itself makes the readout a one-term identity — perfectly sparse, perfectly useless. Preventing collapse requires explicit structural constraints (atom interpretability, capacity caps, a denominator-degree budget) whose strengths trade off against expressivity with no closed-form setting.

**3. Recovery without the restricted isometry property: the measurement matrix is coherent by construction.** Classical compressive-sensing guarantees (Candès–Romberg–Tao 2006; Donoho 2006) require the design matrix to satisfy a restricted isometry property, achieved with high probability by *random* Gaussian/Bernoulli measurements. The dynamics design matrix Θ has rows that are dictionary atoms evaluated along *one correlated trajectory* on a low-dimensional attractor: its columns (e.g., x², x⁴, x⁶ …, or x and x/(1+x²+y²)) are strongly correlated, so the mutual coherence μ(Θ) is large — often near 1. In this regime ℓ1 minimization has no recovery guarantee and empirically returns wrong supports. Coherence-based bounds (exact recovery if sparsity s < ½(1 + 1/μ)) exist but are *pessimistic*: when μ → 1 the admissible sparsity drops below 1, predicting failure for any nontrivial model. Making recovery work therefore demands actively *reducing* μ — decorrelating the dictionary along the trajectory via preconditioning/whitening or orthogonalization — without distorting the symbolic identity of the atoms one is trying to recover. Decorrelation and interpretability pull in opposite directions.

**4. The implicit (null-space) formulation needed for rational/composed atoms introduces a trivial-solution and a noise-amplification problem.** To handle a rational form f = N(x)/D(x) one rewrites it implicitly as D(x)·f − N(x) = 0 and seeks a sparse vector in the null space of a combined library (Mangan et al. 2016; Kaheman et al., SINDy-PI 2020). But the all-zero vector is always in the null space, and the formulation is invariant to scaling, so one must pin a normalization that does not bias the support. Worse, the implicit library contains products of measured derivatives with measured states (D(x)·ẋ), so measurement noise enters *multiplicatively* and is amplified; a method that is exact on clean data can degrade catastrophically at modest noise. The discrete-time map case (Ikeda) is milder than the continuous-derivative case but still requires robust null-space estimation under coherence.

**5. In-sample fit does not determine the attractor: non-uniqueness is real and consequential.** Because the search ranges over bases, several compact models can match the observed trajectory to within noise yet have *different* long-term invariants — a different largest Lyapunov exponent, a different correlation dimension, a spurious attractor or a missing one. Short-horizon prediction error, the default selection criterion, cannot distinguish them: two models can agree for many steps and then diverge onto qualitatively different attractors. Selecting the *dynamically correct* model requires comparing reconstructed invariants against invariants estimated directly from data (Grassberger–Procaccia dimension; Lyapunov spectra from time series), each of which is itself noisy and finite-sample biased. The selection criterion is thus a noisy comparison between two estimates, not a clean oracle.

**6. Validating "it actually discovered structure" rather than "it fit the data" demands controls that are easy to get wrong.** A learned-basis method that improves forecasting could be exploiting extra capacity rather than genuine adaptive sparsification — exactly the confound that makes results uninterpretable. The only defense is a control family: a *random* dictionary of matched size and matched coherence that must NOT recover the equations, and a recovery boundary case in which the method must collapse *exactly* to standard sparse optimization on truly sparse systems. Constructing a coherence-matched random control is subtle: naively random atoms have *lower* coherence than the structured true dictionary, so the placebo must be coherence-matched, and any signal it carries must be shuffled consistently across every place the dictionary feeds (recovery solver, preconditioner, and selection) or the control silently leaks structure.

**Why naive approaches fail, in one line each.** Bigger polynomial dictionary → still dense (transcendental obstruction). Pure rational dictionary → Ikeda's cos(rational) is not rational. Flexible learned lifting alone → memorizes / collapses to trivial sparsifier. ℓ1 on the trajectory dictionary → no RIP, large μ, wrong support. Short-horizon prediction-error selection → picks a model with the wrong attractor. More capacity without controls → improvement is unattributable.

---

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Four barriers — a paradigm assumption, a tooling gap, a theory mismatch left unrepaired, and the closest prior methods stopping just short — explain why the dense regime remains open.

**The paradigm fixed the basis and never questioned it.** From its origin (Wang et al. 2011) through SINDy (Brunton et al. 2016) and its many descendants, the method *is* "choose a dictionary, then enforce sparsity." The dictionary is a hyperparameter set once by the practitioner — polynomials of some degree, or a few trig terms — and the entire methodological apparatus is built around recovering coefficients *within* it. Asking "in which basis is this system sparse?" is a different and harder question that the paradigm's framing does not naturally pose. Consequently the Ikeda map is reported as a *limit* of the approach rather than as a target for a basis search: the failure is attributed to the system ("dense coefficient vector"), not to the basis choice. This framing is sticky because for the showcase systems (Lorenz, Rössler) the polynomial basis is the right one, so the question never had to be asked.

**The enabling tools matured only recently and in a different community.** Three ingredients needed to co-design basis and recovery arrived late and separately. (i) *Implicit / rational sparse identification* — handling f = N/D via a null-space formulation — was introduced for biological networks (Mangan, Brunton, Proctor & Kutz 2016) and made robust as SINDy-PI only in 2020 (Kaheman, Kutz & Brunton). (ii) *Learned dictionaries / Koopman observables* — using a network to discover the functions in which dynamics become simple — came from the deep-Koopman line (Lusch, Kutz & Brunton, *Nat. Commun.* 2018; Williams, Kevrekidis & Rowley, EDMD, 2015) and the coordinate-and-equation autoencoder of Champion, Lusch, Kutz & Brunton (*PNAS* 2019). (iii) *Coherence-based recovery* (s < ½(1+1/μ)) is older (Donoho–Elad 2003; Tropp 2004) but lives in signal processing and was essentially never connected to the *measurement-coherence* objection that the dynamics community itself raised. No prior work assembled all three around the dense-coefficient problem.

**The theory mismatch was acknowledged but not repaired.** The Introduction's remark — that compressive sensing assumes a random projection matrix whereas the power-series formulation yields a structured one — is, to our knowledge, rarely if ever turned into a constructive recovery procedure within the dynamics literature. The standard response has been to hope RIP "approximately" holds or to rely on empirical success on sparse systems where coherence happens to be tolerable. Treating coherence as a *first-class, measured quantity* — reporting μ(Θ), actively reducing it by decorrelating preconditioners, and certifying recovery against the coherence bound — is the step that has been skipped. Without it, the dense regime (where μ → 1) was simply outside any guarantee, and there was no diagnostic to say whether failure came from density or from coherence.

**The closest prior methods stop one structural step short — and that step is our central claim.** Two lines of work come nearest, and naming them sharply is essential. *SINDy-PI / implicit-SINDy* (Mangan 2016; Kaheman 2020) sparsify *rational* nonlinearities but (a) use a *fixed* rational dictionary — so they do not reach cos(rational) Ikeda structure; (b) inherit ℓ1-style recovery with no coherence treatment; and (c) report a single model with no invariant-consistency selection. *SINDy-Autoencoders* (Champion et al. 2019) learn a *coordinate transformation* into a latent space where the dynamics are sparse — the single most related idea — but (a) they transform the *state*, not the *measurement dictionary*, and offer no recovery guarantee tied to the design matrix's coherence; (b) they target clean, low-noise systems and PDE-reduced coordinates, not the polynomial-dense Ikeda class explicitly flagged as the failure case; (c) they provide neither a basis-density spectrum nor a coherence meter, so they cannot *diagnose* which defect (basis vs measurement) caused a failure; and (d) they do not address model non-uniqueness via invariants. Our contribution is precisely the step they omit: we treat the *dictionary and the measurement operator as the objects to co-design*, equip recovery with a coherence-aware guarantee that matches the structured design matrix, and resolve the resulting non-uniqueness with invariant-consistency selection. Where Champion et al. ask "what coordinates make the dynamics simple?", we ask the sharper, measurement-grounded question "what basis makes the system sparse *and* keeps the design matrix recoverable, and which among the equally-good answers has the right attractor?" Following the discipline of demoting the strongest prior art to a baseline: SINDy-Autoencoders becomes one of our comparison methods and, in its random-coordinate variant, part of our placebo, so that "coherence-aware basis discovery beats coordinate-only transformation" is the central falsifiable delta rather than an asserted advantage.

---

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

We propose **DARE — Dictionary-Adaptive Recovery of Equations**: a method that *discovers the basis in which a dense system is sparse*, *recovers the model with a coherence-aware guarantee matched to the structured trajectory design matrix*, and *selects among non-unique fits by dynamical-invariant consistency*. The organizing thesis is that the Ikeda-class failure is the superposition of two separable defects — basis mismatch (C1) and measurement coherence (C2) — and DARE attacks each, with a diagnostic that measures both.

### Method

**Stage 0 — Diagnostics (the basis-density spectrum and coherence meter).** Given a scalar/vector time series (or full state samples), for each candidate dictionary family 𝓕 ∈ {polynomial, Fourier, rational, trig-of-rational, learned} we estimate the *model density* ρ(𝓕) = minimal number of atoms needed to fit within tolerance, and the *mutual coherence* μ(Θ_𝓕) of the resulting design matrix. The pair (ρ, μ) plotted across families is the **basis-density spectrum**; it tells us, before any recovery, whether a system is basis-mismatched (ρ high for polynomials, low for some other family), coherence-limited (μ → 1), or genuinely dense (ρ high for all tractable families). This diagnostic is a standalone contribution.

**Stage 1 — Adaptive composed dictionary.** DARE builds atoms by composition: a learned feature map produces a small set of *interpretable* scalar features g_j(x) = (rational of low degree) — e.g., x, y, 1/(1+x²+y²), x·y — and a fixed outer set of *link functions* L ∈ {identity, (·)², cos, sin, ratio} is applied, yielding composed atoms L(g_j) such as cos(g_j), sin(g_j), g_j/g_k. The rational features are parameterized so their numerator/denominator monomial supports are *learnable but degree-budgeted* (denominator degree ≤ d_D). This is the smallest extension of a dictionary that contains the Ikeda atoms cos(p − k·(1/(1+x²+y²))) while remaining symbolic. Two modes:
- **DARE-Rational** (exact-recovery mode): outer links restricted to {identity, ratio}; pure implicit rational identification, exact when the true system is a low-order rational.
- **DARE-Learn** (composed mode): full link set with the learned rational features; targets trig-of-rational systems such as Ikeda.

**Stage 2 — Coherence-aware recovery.** With the candidate atom library Θ assembled along the trajectory, DARE (i) applies a **decorrelating preconditioner** P (trajectory-whitening / Gram–Schmidt on Θ's columns) to reduce μ(PΘ), recording the achieved coherence; (ii) solves a **mutual-coherence-reweighted** sparse problem — iteratively reweighted ℓ1 with weights informed by inter-atom coherence so that near-duplicate atoms are not double-counted — in the implicit null-space form D(x)·f − N(x) = 0 with a fixed normalization ‖[coefficients]‖ pinned to avoid the trivial solution; and (iii) **certifies** each recovered model against the coherence bound by reporting whether its support size s satisfies s < ½(1 + 1/μ(PΘ)), labeling recoveries inside the bound [provable-by-construction] and outside it [conjectured/empirical]. This is the step that operationalizes the Introduction's structured-matrix objection.

**Stage 3 — Invariant-consistency selection.** DARE retains the *set* of admissible sparse models (those within fit tolerance) and selects the one minimizing a weighted discrepancy between model-reconstructed and data-estimated invariants: largest Lyapunov exponent λ₁, correlation dimension D₂ (Grassberger–Procaccia), and the power spectrum (Wasserstein distance between spectra). Ties broken by description length. The retained alternatives and their invariant discrepancies are reported, making non-uniqueness explicit and auditable.

**Network extension (DARE-Net).** For *coupled networks* of dense-local-dynamics nodes (e.g., a ring/lattice of Ikeda or laser maps with diffusive or sinusoidal coupling), DARE shares the learned composed dictionary across nodes and infers the coupling graph by message passing on a graph neural network (P-GNN/ID-GNN-style positional encodings), with edge inclusion pruned by causation-entropy (Sun & Bollt) to suppress indirect-coupling false positives. Boundary cases: single node → DARE; known graph → per-node DARE. This extension is where synchronization structure (cluster/group sync) and GNN expressivity enter; it is evaluated as an extension, not a precondition for the core claim.

### Datasets / testbeds (three difficulty tiers, all simulated → cheap, reproducible)

| Tier | System | Why | DARE expectation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sparse (control) | Lorenz, Rössler (flows); standard map (Fourier-sparse) | Recovery anchor | **must equal** SINDy / compressive sensing within ε |
| Rationally-sparse | Michaelis–Menten / rational recurrences; a 1/(1+x²+y²)-linear map | Exact mechanism demo | **exact** symbolic recovery (DARE-Rational) |
| Composed-dense | **Ikeda map** (a,b,k,p as in the Introduction); driven-damped pendulum (sinusoidal); a laser-cavity variant | The target wall | **orders-of-magnitude** lower density + better invariants than fixed-basis [conjectured] |
| Network | Ring of N coupled Ikeda/laser maps | Structure + dense locals | recover coupling graph + local dynamics |
| Honest-fail | A system with no compact representation in any tractable family | Limitation probe | DARE falls back to predictive (Koopman/reservoir) model, reports symbolic failure |

All systems are studied at multiple data lengths and additive-noise levels (0, 1%, 5%, 10%); the discrete-time map systems use exact next-state pairs, the flows use finite-difference or weak-form derivatives to mitigate multiplicative noise amplification.

### Baselines and ablations (all capacity- and dictionary-size-matched)

| Model | Role |
|---|---|
| SINDy / Wang–Lai compressive sensing (fixed polynomial+Fourier) | Primary baseline; expected to **fail** on Ikeda (reproduce the Introduction's claim quantitatively) |
| SINDy-PI / implicit-SINDy (fixed rational) | Strong baseline; handles rational but not cos(rational) |
| SINDy-Autoencoders (Champion 2019) | Strongest prior art; coordinate-transform comparison |
| Reservoir computer / neural ODE | Non-interpretable forecasting ceiling (invariants only) |
| **DARE-Rational**, **DARE-Learn**, **DARE-Net** | Proposed |
| DARE − coherence (vanilla ℓ1) | Ablation: isolates pillar 2 |
| DARE − invariant-selection (prediction-error only) | Ablation: isolates pillar 3 |
| DARE − learned features (fixed composed atoms) | Ablation: isolates pillar 1 |

### Metrics

Symbolic-support correctness (did it find the right atoms? — exact tiers); **model density** ρ vs basis-density-spectrum prediction; **coherence** μ(PΘ) achieved and whether recovery sits below s < ½(1+1/μ); valid-prediction time / forecast horizon; **invariant errors** |Δλ₁|, |ΔD₂|, spectral Wasserstein distance; robustness curves vs noise and data length; for DARE-Net, coupling-graph F1 and false-indirect-edge rate (causation-entropy gate on/off).

### Eval ladder (falsifiers; pre-registered)

- **Oracle.** Supply the true atom support (e.g., the Ikeda numerator/denominator + cos/sin links). Upper bound on recovery; DARE ≤ Oracle by construction. *Controls:* whether the system is recoverable in principle given the right basis.
- **Placebo [central falsifier].** Replace the learned/composed dictionary with a **random dictionary of matched atom count and matched mutual coherence**, with any conditioning shuffled *consistently* across preconditioner, recovery solver, and selection. *Required outcome:* the placebo does NOT recover Ikeda structure — Spearman ρ between recovered and true coefficients is statistically indistinguishable from 0 (|ρ| < 0.1, permutation p > 0.05), and its forecast/invariants do not beat the fixed-basis baseline. If the matched-coherence random dictionary recovers nearly as well (|ρ| > 0.5), the *adaptive-basis* claim is retracted and the result reframed as a capacity effect.
- **Recovery [provable-by-construction].** On the sparse tier, DARE's learned denominator → constant (D ≡ 1) and links → identity, so DARE reduces *exactly* to SINDy / compressive sensing; recovered support and coefficients must match the fixed-basis method within ε (e.g., relative coefficient error < 0.1%). The rational dictionary with D ≡ 1 algebraically *contains* the polynomial dictionary, so non-inferiority is structural; the test verifies optimization realizes it.
- **Leakage (OOD).** Discover on one Ikeda parameter set (a,b,k,p) and one coupling regime; test symbolic-form transfer and invariant prediction on *disjoint* parameter sets and on a held-out dense system. Recovering the *functional form* (not just refit coefficients) on unseen parameters is genuine generalization; coefficient memorization is not.
- **Do-no-harm.** DARE must not degrade recovery on the sparse control tier beyond a pre-registered ε non-inferiority floor (relative coefficient error and forecast horizon), guaranteeing the dense-regime capability costs nothing on the systems sparse optimization already solves.

### Expected outcomes (epistemic labels applied)

1. **[provable-by-construction]** DARE's hypothesis class contains the fixed-basis class: with D ≡ 1 and identity links, DARE *is* SINDy, so it cannot be strictly less expressive; **[conjectured]** the trained DARE reproduces SINDy on Lorenz/Rössler/standard map within ε (deviation flags an optimization or matching bug).
2. **[provable-by-construction]** On the rationally-sparse tier, DARE-Rational's implicit dictionary contains the true atoms, so exact recovery is achievable; **[conjectured]** it is realized at ≤ 5% noise.
3. **[conjectured]** On the **Ikeda map**, fixed-basis sparse optimization fails (coefficient vector density ≫ data, support unrecoverable — *we reproduce the Introduction's claim quantitatively*), while DARE-Learn attains an order-of-magnitude smaller model density and substantially longer valid-prediction time, with |Δλ₁| and |ΔD₂| within pre-registered tolerances. We explicitly do **not** claim exact symbolic recovery of cos(p − k/(1+x²+y²)) in general — only compact, invariant-faithful approximation — and we report when the true composed form *is* recovered as a bonus.
4. **[provable-by-construction — falsifier]** The matched-coherence random-dictionary **placebo does not track** the true coefficients (|ρ| < 0.1, p > 0.05); a positive placebo retracts the adaptive-basis claim. This is the hard pass/fail gate separating "discovered structure" from "more capacity."
5. **[conjectured]** Ablating coherence-aware recovery (vanilla ℓ1) degrades support recovery sharply as μ(Θ) → 1, isolating pillar 2; ablating invariant-selection yields models with correct short-horizon error but wrong λ₁/D₂ (a different attractor), demonstrating and then resolving non-uniqueness — pillar 3.
6. **[conjectured]** DARE-Net recovers the coupling graph of a ring of coupled Ikeda maps with high F1, and the causation-entropy gate cuts false indirect edges relative to a correlation-only baseline; boundary cases (single node, known graph) reduce to DARE.
7. **[conjectured]** On the honest-fail system, DARE reports symbolic failure (no compact fit below the coherence bound) and falls back to a Koopman/reservoir predictor that still matches invariants — establishing the method's *honest scope* rather than overclaiming universality.

### Responsible-AI / safety considerations

The work is methodological and uses only simulated dynamical systems, so direct risk is low, but three considerations are folded in. (a) **Deployment over-trust / non-uniqueness:** because many compact models fit the same data, a discovered equation must never be presented as *the* mechanism without its invariant-consistency evidence and its reported alternatives; DARE's selection step and the explicit alternative-set reporting are the safeguard, important precisely because these tools feed high-stakes forecasts (climate tipping points, laser-cavity stability). (b) **Dual-use:** the Ikeda map literally models a laser cavity, and equation discovery from observation has system-identification implications for physical/optical and infrastructure systems; we restrict released testbeds to standard published benchmarks and discuss, rather than enable, sensitive identification. (c) **Reproducibility and honest scope:** every claim carries an epistemic label, all thresholds are pre-registered, the coherence certificate is reported per model, and the honest-fail tier is a required experiment so the paper's *limits* are demonstrated, not hidden. Compute footprint is modest (small simulated systems, lightweight learned features), so environmental cost is negligible.
